then raced away (COMMENT: This report doubtful, due to nearby mill
#3 FEB.1, 1953. Morning. GEORGETOWN BAY, TASMANIA. One flyi
saucer rotated as it noiselessly crossed sky at terrific speed at
feet. (COMMENT: Tasmania is south of Australia, and not near any k

#4 FEB. 2, 1953. Noon. PASADENA, CALIFORNIA. 42 1/8°, 118 1/4°. One ver
brilliant disk with a hole in center hovered for 1 or 2 minutes no
ly, then disappeared. (COMMENT: This is a very rare saucer shape,
was reported occasionally in 1947 when saucers were first seen.)
#5 FEB. 2, 1953. 5:00 P.M. HALIFAX, NOVA SCOTIA, CANADA. Avery long
white streak crossed sky, but investigation located no known aircra
#6 FEB. 3 1953. Afternoon. WINSLOW, ARIZONA. 35°, 110 3/4° Three sil
objects flying close together changed to red color, then back to si
as they crossed sky and disappeared in a few seconds.
#7 FEB. 3 1953. 4:15 PM. VILLE ST. LAURENT, QUEBEC, CANADA. 45 1/2°,
Tremendous explosion in sky broke windows within 6 miles. Only 2 kn
aircraft were not doing sonic speeds or approaching them.
#8 FEB. 3 1953. 5:02 to 5:07 PM. SUDBURY, ONTARIO. 46 1/2°, 81°. A sil
object, 1/2 the size of the full moon, remained motionless in sky for
minutes. It was elongated like a barrage balloon, but none were in
#9 FEB. 3, 1953. 7:30 P.M. NORTH ATLANTIC OCEAN. A brilliant flare
from ocean. No aircraft survivors were ever found in area where air
crashed.
#10 FEB. 3 1953. 11:20 P.M. HARTFORD (AND NEARBY COUNTIES), CONN.
A bright flash of light in sky, followed by a brilliant ball of fire
lasted several seconds. Three minutes later, a huge explosion in sk
rocked area. (COMMENT: This appears to be an exceptionally large me

to the mysterious rising 'flare' seen 3 1/2 hours earlier in the Atlan
#11 FEB. 3, 1953. Evening. LEXINGTON, OREGON. 45 1/2°, 119 1/2°. An obje
appeared to throw off blue light crossed sky. (COMMENT: Although a
many flights of balloons, the blue color and after dark observatio
tend to back this being an unknown object, as the sunset makes ball
appear red.)
#12 FEB. 4? 1953. Evening?? SAUGERTIES, NEW YORK. A reddish light
in sky. No details available.
#13 FEB. 5? 1953. Afternoon. MORBIERE, FRANCE. 47 1/2°, 5°. Brillia
or bright spot appeared in a vapor trail after the airplane crosse
leaving a huge trail, and remained in same place after winds blew
trail away.
